title: Field-induced multipolar character in the dipolar ground state of the honeycomb rare-earth chalcohalide NdOF
authors: Tiantian Liu, Yanzhen Cai, Mingtai Xie, Helin Mei, Anmin Zhang, Feng Jin, Jianting Ji, Zheng Zhang, Qingming Zhang
abstract: Field-tunable reconstruction of crystalline electric field (CEF) doublets offers a promising avenue for inducing multipolar character, while its observation in real materials has been little explored so far. Here we establish the honeycomb rare-earth chalcohalide NdOF as such a platform. Raman spectroscopy identifies four CEF excitations at 1.7, 15.6, 19.2, and 80.9~meV, and a Zeeman--CEF analysis reproduces their nonlinear field splitting into seven branches. Magnetization and susceptibility over 0.1--9~T are well described by a CEF model for the total angular momentum $J = 9/2$ manifold, confirming the robustness of the extracted CEF scheme. These results demonstrate a field-driven continuous evolution of the ground-state doublet from dipolar to dipolar-multipolar character, with pressure providing a complementary tuning knob, establishing NdOF as a model system for exploring the controlled induction of multipolar components in rare-earth magnets.
summary: This work investigates NdOF, a rare-earth magnet, to show that its ground state can be controlled by external magnetic fields. By combining Raman spectroscopy and magnetization measurements with a CEF model, the authors prove that the ground state evolves from a simple dipolar state to a complex dipolar-multipolar state. This establishes NdOF as a key platform for studying the controlled induction of higher-order magnetic multipolar moments.
main_problem: The research aims to understand and control the transition of the ground state in rare-earth magnets from a purely dipolar character to a mixed dipolar-multipolar character.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the ground state doublet in NdOF can be continuously tuned from dipolar to dipolar-multipolar by applying external magnetic fields, establishing the material as a model system for studying multipolar interactions.
method: The study combines experimental techniques like Raman spectroscopy and magnetization measurements with theoretical modeling using the Crystal Electric Field (CEF) Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The physical system is the honeycomb rare-earth chalcohalide NdOF, where the magnetic properties are governed by the CEF Hamiltonian acting on the $J=9/2$ manifold of the Nd$^{3+}$ ion. The Hamiltonian includes terms up to the sixth power of Stevens operators, constrained by local $C_{3v}$ symmetry.
key_observables: CEF excitation energies (1.7, 15.6, 19.2, 80.9 meV), magnetization ($M$), susceptibility ($\chi$), and the ratio of dipolar-octupolar moments.
important_parameters: External magnetic field strength (0.1–9 T) and temperature (low T regime).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the structure remains stable and that the observed excitations are electronic in origin, allowing the use of the CEF model.
figures_summary: Figures show the crystal structure and low-temperature Raman spectra revealing four intrinsic CEF peaks. Further figures illustrate the field-induced nonlinear splitting of these excitations and the temperature/field dependence of magnetization matching the CEF model.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the material and the CEF model using Raman spectroscopy and magnetization measurements. It then uses the field dependence of the excitations and magnetic data to show that the ground state undergoes a continuous evolution from dipolar to multipolar character, controlled by the external field.
why_interesting: The field-tunable induction of multipolar character is highly relevant to AMO physics and open quantum systems, as it demonstrates how external fields can drive a quantum system's ground state into a complex, non-simple magnetic state, which can be modeled using effective Hamiltonians.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal structure, phase purity, and lattice vibrations of NdOF. (a) Crystal structure of NdOF, which adopts the SmSI-type trigonal structure (R3m). The Nd3+ ion (blue) is coordinated by four O (red) and four F (purple) atoms forming a bicapped trigonal antiprism with local C3v symmetry. The bottom panel shows the two-dimensional honeycomb arrangement of Nd3+ ions in the ab plane. (b) Rietveld refinement of powder X-ray diffraction data collected at 295 K and 3 K. The observed (blue circles), calculated (red line), and difference (green line) profiles are shown, with vertical bars marking the Bragg-peak positions. The results confirm the single-phase nature of NdOF and the absence...

FIG. 2. CEF excitations of NdOF revealed by Raman scattering. (a) Temperature-dependent Raman spectra measured between 150 K and 1.7 K with 473 nm excitation. At low temperature four sharp intrinsic CEF peaks (PCEF1 – PCEF4) are observed, corresponding to transitions from the ground-state doublet. Additional weaker non-intrinsic features arising from transitions between thermally populated excited doublets are visible at elevated temperatures but vanish upon cooling. (b) Extracted peak positions of the four intrinsic CEF excitations as a function of temperature. The intrinsic transitions remain essentially temperature independent. (c) Schematic CEF level scheme of Nd3+ (J = 9/2) in NdOF....

FIG. 3. Magnetic-field evolution of CEF excitations in NdOF. (a) Raman spectra measured at 1.8 K with 473 nm excitation un- der fields up to 9 T. The four intrinsic CEF peaks split and shift with increasing field, revealing multiple branches at high fields. (b) Extracted peak positions as a function of field. Seven distinct tran- sitions are resolved, corresponding to excitations from the ground- state doublet to field-split excited states, in quantitative agreement with the CEF+Zeeman analysis. (c) Calculated Zeeman splitting of the J = 9/2 manifold for powder-averaged samples, compared with single-crystal calculations for H ∥c and H ⊥c. The results show nearly linear splitting for fields...

FIG. 4. M/H-T and M-H of NdOF and comparison with CEF modeling. (a) – (d) M/H-T under fields of 0.1, 3, 5, and 9 T. The CEF simulations (blue solid lines) reproduce the experimental data (symbols) over the entire range. (e), (f) M-H at 1.8 and 10 K. The CEF model (blue solid lines) is in good agreement with experiment. (g), (h) Calculated CEF magnetization for fields along the c-axis and within the ab-plane at 1.8 K.
